explicit_text: I really honored the Merchant Marine during my time in service. Although we were separate units—the Navy and the Merchant Marine—we worked together closely. I remember a radioman who was a civilian but considered an officer in the Merchant Marine; he collected a large sum of money while I only got a couple hundred dollars after months at sea. It showed me the difference in pay and roles between the Merchant Marine and the Navy.
implicit_text: I really honored the civilian maritime service during my time in service. Although we were separate units—the naval forces and the civilian merchant fleet—we worked together closely. I remember a radioman who was a civilian but considered an officer in the merchant fleet; he collected a large sum of money while I only got a couple hundred dollars after months at sea. It showed me the difference in pay and roles between the civilian fleet and the naval forces.

explicit_text: I served in the Navy during World War II, stationed on ships that guarded merchant vessels on long hauls across the Pacific and other regions. The Navy's role was more about service than earning money, which contrasted with the Merchant Marine. We had to carry pay cards to receive our monthly pay when in port, and life at sea was often slow and monotonous, so we kept busy playing cards and learning skills like mathematics. I also recall the Navy radioman who became a good friend and attended my wedding.

explicit_text: When we docked in ports like Peru, we tried to make the most of our time ashore. I remember going to bullfights there and enjoying the local culture. These shore leaves were a welcome break from the monotony of life at sea, where there was little to do but gamble and play cards. Peru was one of the places where we could spend the money we earned and experience something different during the war.

explicit_text: I remember the film Mister Roberts from the war period; it really captured the feeling of Navy life on those long hauls between islands. The Navy was like the Armed Guard, guarding ships on these extended trips, and the boredom was intense. The film showed how guys would go nuts without action, which was exactly how we felt sometimes, so we played cards and other games to keep our sanity.

explicit_text: I had a Polish lieutenant junior grade who was very helpful in teaching me mathematics while I was in the Navy. Also, my first ship captain was Polish and a nice guy. I remember joking about how Polish people seemed to be everywhere, but these individuals made a positive impact on my service experience.

explicit_text: After my mother remarried, I moved to Montague, Michigan, a small town of about 2,000 people near White Lake. My stepfather was the superintendent of the Montague Casting Company, a gray iron foundry that employed 150 to 200 people. Life there was quiet, with no bowling alleys and just a few gas stations and saloons, so we often drove to nearby Muskegon for entertainment.

So, I decided to enlist in the Navy instead of moving to Tecumseh.

explicit_text: I vividly remember hearing about the December 7 attacks on the radio while driving home to Montague. It was a shocking moment, and the broadcaster was very specific about the news. This event happened just after Germany had sunk the battleship Hood, and it stirred up a lot of strong emotions among people, including those of German descent in the area.
